cards.grammarNote: لا (la) + present tense verb = negates the verb. لا أفهم (I don't understand), لا أعرف (I don't know), لا أحب (I don't like). This is the most basic negation pattern for present-tense verbs.
لا (la) as a standalone word = no. The opposite of نعم (na'am = yes). لا alone is direct; adding شكراً softens the refusal.
لا before a noun (not a verb) = 'there is no...' لا مشكلة (no problem), لا وقت (no time), لا مكان (no place). This is a different grammatical use of لا from the verbal negation.
ما (ma) is used in Levantine Arabic to negate past-tense verbs. Very common in everyday speech: ما أكلت (I didn't eat), ما عرفت (I didn't know), ما جيت (I didn't come). In MSA, past negation uses لم.
ما في (ma fi) = there is no... in Levantine Arabic. Extremely common. ما في حدا (no one is here), ما في وقت (there's no time). This is equivalent to MSA لا يوجد (la yujad).

لم (lam) + present-tense verb form = past negation in MSA. The verb after لم uses the present form but means past: لم أذهب (I did not go), لم يأكل (he did not eat). لم is formal — you'll see it in news, writing, and formal speech.
لن (lan) + present verb = will not (future negation). لن أذهب (I will not go), لن يأتي (he will not come). Used in both MSA and educated speech.

cards.front: He is not a doctor. (formal — laysa)
cards.grammarNote: ليس (laysa) = is not, negates equational sentences formally. ليس طبيباً (he is not a doctor — note the accusative ending on طبيباً). Feminine: ليست (laysat). Colloquial alternative: مش (mish) in Levantine.

مش (mish) = not, negates adjectives and nouns in Levantine colloquial Arabic. مش كويس (mish kuwayyis = not good), مش هون (mish hun = not here), مش صحيح (mish sahih = not correct). Very frequent in everyday Levantine speech.

أبداً (abadan) = never, used after لا + verb: لا أتأخر أبداً (I never arrive late). As a standalone answer: أبداً! = Absolutely not!/Never! Also: لم + verb + قط (qatt) = never did in the past: لم أذهب قط (I have never gone).
